text: The surgeon uses the robotic arms to take down adhesions to the anterior abdominal wall and begins dissection at the sacral promontory, detaching peritoneal attachments medially. The robotic arm grasps the colon, retracting it anteriorly and laterally, providing counter traction for dissection. The robotic camera view shows the rectum and mesentery taut, facilitating smooth dissection cephalad. The third robotic arm maintains counter tension along the sigmoid colon, aiding in the identification of the left ureter and enabling high ligation of the vascular pedicle with a vessel sealer.

The surgeon uses the robotic arm to grasp and retract the colon anteriorly and laterally, providing counter traction for dissection. The robotic camera view shows the rectum and its mesentery taut, facilitating smooth dissection cephalad from the sacral promontory, as peritoneal attachments are dissected medially.

The third robotic arm is used to apply counter tension on the sigmoid colon, facilitating proximal dissection. The robotic camera view shows the colon retracted anteriorly and laterally, aiding in the identification of the left ureter. The surgeon completes proximal dissection with high ligation of the vascular pedicle using a vessel sealer. The retroperitoneal attachments of the sigmoid colon are taken down, ensuring the mesentery is lifted while the left ureter and gonadal vessels remain below. The ascending colon is mobilized laterally along the white line of TOLT up to the splenic flexure.

The surgeon uses robotic assistance to take down the retroperitoneal attachments of the sigmoid colon, ensuring the mesentery is lifted upwards while the left ureter and gonadal vessels remain down. The ascending colon is mobilized laterally along the white line of Toldt up to the splenic flexure. The third robotic arm lifts the rectum and mesorectum anteriorly, creating a window to transect the colon distally at the rectosigmoid junction with a robotic stapler. The mesorectum is dissected and divided at the level of the promontory, including division of the superior hemorrhoidal vessels. Adequate length and blood supply to the colon are confirmed before opening the colon and placing an EEA anvil into the descending colon.

The third robotic arm lifts the rectum and mesorectum anteriorly, creating a window for transection at the rectosigmoid junction using a robotic stapler. The surgeon then dissects and divides the mesorectum at the level of the sacral promontory, including division of the superior hemorrhoidal vessels.

The surgeon verifies the adequate length and blood supply to the colon before opening it. An EEA anvil is inserted into the descending colon, and the colotomy is closed with sutures. Subsequently, the colon is transected proximally, and a stapled EEA anastomosis is performed.
The surgeon closes the colotomy with sutures, followed by proximal transection of the colon. A stapled end-to-end anastomosis (EEA) is performed to connect the transected ends of the colon.

The surgeon checks the integrity of the anastomosis by inspecting the donuts and performing an air leak test. The surgical specimen is extracted through a right lower quadrant incision. Trocars are removed under direct vision, and fascial defects larger than ten millimeters are closed.
